questions.question: Explain the three categories of permissions in Linux and what each category controls.
keyPoints: Owner/User permissions - controls access for the file owner
Group permissions - controls access for members of the file's group
Others permissions - controls access for all other users
Each category can have Read, Write, and Execute permissions

questions.question: Differentiate between symbolic mode and numeric mode in the chmod command with examples.
keyPoints: Symbolic mode uses letters: u (owner), g (group), o (others), a (all) with operators (+, -, =)
Numeric mode uses octal numbers: 4=read, 2=write, 1=execute
Example symbolic: chmod u+x file.sh (adds execute for owner)
Example numeric: chmod 755 file.sh (sets rwxr-xr-x)
Symbolic is more precise, numeric is more concise

questions.question: What is umask? How is it used to calculate default permissions for files and directories?
keyPoints: umask (User File Creation Mode Mask) is a value that determines default permissions
Default file permission: 666 (rw-rw-rw-) minus umask
Default directory permission: 777 (rwxrwxrwx) minus umask
Example with umask 022: Files get 644, Directories get 755
umask can be changed using umask command

questions.question: Explain the three special permissions in Linux: SetUID, SetGID, and Sticky Bit with their numeric values and use cases.
keyPoints: SetUID (4000): Makes executable run with owner's privileges, shown as 's' in owner position
SetGID (2000): Makes executable run with group's privileges OR directories inherit group, shown as 's' in group position
Sticky Bit (1000): On directories, only owner can delete files, shown as 't' in others position
Use cases: SetUID for passwd command, SetGID for shared directories, Sticky Bit for /tmp
Applied like: chmod 4755 file (SetUID + rwxr-xr-x)

questions.question: Write the difference between chmod +x and chmod 755 commands.
keyPoints: chmod +x adds execute permission to owner, group, and others (makes it a=rx)
chmod 755 explicitly sets owner=rwx (7), group=rx (5), others=rx (5)
chmod +x is equivalent to chmod a+x
chmod 755 gives owner more permissions (write) than +x
Use +x for making scripts executable, use 755 for precise control

questions.question: How would you secure a sensitive file containing passwords? What permissions would you set and why?
keyPoints: Set permissions to 600 (rw-------) using: chmod 600 filename
This gives owner read and write (6), group and others get no permissions (0)
Only the owner can access the file
Prevents other users from reading sensitive data
For extra security, consider file encryption in addition to permissions

questions.question: Explain the output of ls -l command with respect to file permissions. What does -rw-r--r-- indicate?
keyPoints: First character indicates file type (- for regular file, d for directory)
Next 9 characters show permissions in 3 groups of 3
rw- = owner has read and write (not execute)
r-- = group has read only
r-- = others have read only
Total numeric value: 6-4-4 = 644

questions.question: Explain the file permission system in Linux/Unix in detail. Discuss the types of permissions, representation methods, and how to modify them with chmod command.
keyPoints: Three types: Owner, Group, Others - each can have Read, Write, Execute
Two representation methods: Symbolic (rwx) and Numeric (octal 0-7)
chmod command: chmod [options] mode file
Symbolic mode: u/g/o/a +/- r/w/x, examples: chmod u+x, chmod go-rw
Numeric mode: 4=read, 2=write, 1=execute, combine by adding
Common permissions: 644 (files), 755 (scripts/programs), 600 (private), 700 (private dir)
Special permissions: SetUID(4000), SetGID(2000), Sticky Bit(1000)
Recursive changes: chmod -R
Practical examples and security implications

questions.question: Describe special permissions (SetUID, SetGID, Sticky Bit) in detail with practical examples and security implications.
keyPoints: SetUID (4000): When set on executable, process runs with owner's UID, example: /usr/bin/passwd has -rwSr-xr-x
Security concern: Can be exploited if vulnerabilities exist
SetGID (2000): On executables, runs with group's GID; on directories, new files inherit directory's group
Sticky Bit (1000): On directories like /tmp, users can only delete their own files
How to set: chmod 4755 (SetUID), chmod 2755 (SetGID), chmod 1755 (Sticky Bit)
Visual representation: 's' or 'S' for SetUID/SetGID, 't' or 'T' for Sticky Bit
Lowercase means execute permission exists, uppercase means it doesn't
Security best practices: Avoid SetUID on unknown programs, audit regularly

questions.question: A system administrator needs to set up a shared directory where multiple users can create files but only the file owner can delete their own files. Explain how to achieve this with appropriate permissions.
keyPoints: Create directory with appropriate group ownership
Set SetGID (chmod 2770 dir) so new files inherit the group
Add all users to the shared group
Set Sticky Bit (chmod 1770 dir) to prevent users from deleting others' files
Final permissions: 3770 or 1770 depending on requirements
Example: mkdir /shared, chgrp developers /shared, chmod 2775 /shared
Verify with ls -ld /shared showing rwsrwsrwt
Explain each component's purpose in detail
Common use cases: project directories, team collaboration folders

questions.question: Explain umask in detail. How does it work? Calculate the default permissions for files and directories when umask is set to 027.
keyPoints: umask (User File Creation Mode Mask) is a 4-digit octal number
It SUBTRACTS from default permissions to determine new file/directory permissions
Default permissions: Files = 666 (rw-rw-rw-), Directories = 777 (rwxrwxrwx)
Calculation for umask 027: Files: 666-027=640 (rw-r-----), Directories: 777-027=750 (rwxr-x---)
umask can be viewed with 'umask' command and set with 'umask 027'
First digit in umask (0 here) affects special permissions
Common umask values: 022 (default), 077 (maximum privacy), 002 (shared systems)
umask is inherited by child processes
Environment variable: can be set in ~/.bashrc or /etc/profile
